serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of sweetened soy milk is approximately 250 ml (250 g), commonly served as a breakfast or snack beverage in Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Soy Milk — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for sweetened soy milk (beverage made from soaked soya beans, water, and sugar). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this source.

gi_source.title: Soy milk, full-fat — GI value 34 (Atkinson et al., 2008; University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 34 for full-fat soy milk is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (34 × 19.25) / 100 = 6.5, based on a 250 g serving providing 19.25 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.6 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, despite low sodium (14 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sweetened soy milk and choose unsweetened soy milk instead, as the 7.6 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines on sugar-sweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.6 g sugar/100 g and only 0.6 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing postprandial glucose spikes unsuitable for glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this sweetened soy milk entirely and substitute unsweetened soy milk (approximately 1 g sugar/100 g), which has a low GI of 34 and provides beneficial soy protein without the glycaemic burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.6 g/100 g in a beverage raises triglyceride levels and contributes to cardiometabolic risk; however, soy protein (2.6 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g) provide modest cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, this sweetened soy milk is not recommended due to its high added sugar content (7.6 g/100 g); unsweetened soy milk is preferred as soy protein has been associated with modest LDL cholesterol reduction per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.6 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, offsetting the anti-inflammatory potential of soy isoflavones present in the beverage.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened soy milk to benefit from soy isoflavones without the inflammatory burden of 7.6 g added sugar per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweetened soy milk is a plant-based beverage made by soaking dried soya beans, grinding them with water, boiling the mixture, and straining out the solids, then adding sugar to produce a mildly sweet, creamy white drink. It is a staple beverage in Singapore and across East Asia, commonly consumed at breakfast alongside traditional foods such as you tiao (fried dough fritters) or as a mid-morning snack from hawker centres and coffee shops. The flavour is mildly nutty and sweet, with a thin, smooth texture lighter than dairy milk. Per 100 g, sweetened soy milk provides 55 kcal, 2.6 g protein, 1.6 g fat (of which approximately 0.3 g is saturated), 7.7 g carbohydrate (7.6 g sugar), 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 14 mg sodium. It is naturally cholesterol-free, dairy-free, and vegan. The glycemic index is 34 (low), sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database, making the base soy milk glycaemically favourable; however, the added sugar in this sweetened variant raises the glycemic load to approximately 6.5 per 250 ml serving and makes it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. Soy protein in this beverage contains all essential amino acids and soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) with potential anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties, though these benefits are partially offset by the added sugar content. Unsweetened soy milk is the preferred clinical alternative for metabolic health management.
common_user_queries: Is soy milk good for diabetes?
Can I drink soy milk if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in soy milk?
What is the GI of soy milk?
Is soy milk good for heart health?
Soy milk vs dairy milk nutrition
Can I drink soy milk if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI value are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match) and the University of Sydney GI Database respectively. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The food record is canonical, reviewed, and provenance-confirmed.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 0.3 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for soy milk (NDB 16222 / FDC ID 1097542) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; this is documented as an adapted value. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific sweetened soy milk product. Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0 mg/100 g) as soy milk is a plant-based product containing no cholesterol by nature. This is a sweetened variant; unsweetened soy milk has approximately 1 g sugar/100 g and would have different health suitability ratings. GI value of 34 applies to full-fat soy milk from the University of Sydney GI Database; the sweetened formulation may have a marginally higher effective GI due to added sucrose, but no direct GI measurement for sweetened soy milk was found in the approved GI database, so the value of 34 is retained as the best available approved-source estimate with the caveat noted here.
